item.title: Dem Rhode Island lawmaker threatens ICE: 'Your day of reckoning will come m----- f------'
item.description: Rhode Island Democrat Enrique Sanchez threatened ICE with a profane 'day of reckoning' post, escalating his history of calling agents 'Nazi Gestapo thugs.'
item.content:encoded:
A Rhode Island lawmaker who previously referred to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers as "Nazi Gestapo thugs" said "your day of reckoning will come mother------" in a charged social media post aimed at the agency tasked with carrying out the Trump administration's crackdown on illegal immigration.

State Rep. Enrique Sanchez, a Democrat, directed his anger at ICE in a Wednesday post on X decrying its enforcement tactics:

"Fighting and dealing with ICE since last year has f----- me up in the head after trying to protect and defend hundreds and hundreds of our community members in Providence and across Rhode Island. Your day of reckoning will come mother------," he wrote. "I swear to god."

HOMAN BLASTS DEM HYPOCRISY ON DEPORTATIONS, RECALLS PAST WHEN 'OBAMA GAVE ME AN AWARD' FOR ENFORCING BORDER

Fox News Digital has reached out for comment to Sanchez, ICE, and the Department of Homeland Security, along with the Rhode Island Republican and Democratic parties.

Sanchez, who was elected to the state House of Representatives in 2022, works as a case manager helping illegal immigrants, according to his biography on the state House website.

The Democratic lawmaker has issued harsh criticism of ICE before. In August 2025, he called ICE officers "Nazi Gestapo thugs" who he said "kidnapped another of our neighbors" in Providence.

"The ICE thugs damaged a couple cars that belonged to residents as well. They think they are above the law," he wrote on X at the time.

SOCIALIST MAYOR MAMDANI BASHES ICE AFTER CHAOTIC PROTEST LEADS TO ARRESTS: ‘CRUEL AND INHUMANE’

Two weeks later, he said ICE was "terrorizing" communities before calling for "a day of reckoning and no going back."

More recently, Sanchez lambasted the "Nazi ICE trash thugs" after an ICE agent killed a Colombian man in Maine.

Joan Sebastian Guerrero, 26, was shot and killed July 13 during an attempted traffic stop after he allegedly tried to flee, authorities said.

An eyewitness told Maine's Portland Press Herald that Guerrero appeared to drive toward federal agents before shots were fired.

item.title: Alex Murdaugh ranks among South Carolina’s biggest tax deadbeats while awaiting new murder trial
item.description: South Carolina revenue records list Alex Murdaugh's $1.6 million tax debt as his lawyers push to test unknown male DNA found under Maggie's fingernails.
item.content:encoded:
As Alex Murdaugh awaits a new trial in the murders of his wife and son, the disgraced former attorney is facing a separate seven-figure problem.

Murdaugh owes more than $1.6 million in unpaid South Carolina taxes, penalties and interest, ranking him among the state’s biggest individual tax delinquents, according to newly published records.

The South Carolina Department of Revenue (SCDOR) listed "Murdaugh, Richard" as owing $1,630,201.02 in its latest quarterly report of top delinquent taxpayers, published July 16. The debt is the fourth-largest attributed to an individual on the state’s current list.

The agency said it publishes the names and outstanding balances of delinquent taxpayers as part of an effort to promote "transparency, fairness and compliance."

MURDAUGH WANTS IDAHO MURDERS DNA DREAM TEAM BEHIND KOHBERGER CASE TO HELP REWRITE SECOND MURDER TRIAL

Before anyone lands on the public list, revenue officials said they repeatedly attempt to resolve the debt through letters, phone calls or personal contact. A state tax lien has also been filed against everyone named, making each debt a matter of public record.

"We’ve sent letters, made calls, or attempted personal contact multiple times with no resolution," the department states in its online frequently asked questions.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Murdaugh’s seven-figure tax debt comes as his attorneys prepare to defend him in a second murder trial. Dick Harpootlian previously told Fox News Digital that the lawyers received a substantial flat fee upfront roughly five years ago and would remain on the case through its conclusion.

SEND US A TIP

"We got paid a fee up front, you know, five years ago. It was a big fee," he said. "So we're in the case until it's over."

GOT A TIP?

Harpootlian conceded that the yearslong legal battle could prove costly for his firm but said that risk is inherent in his work.

"Sometimes you take cases that turn out to be economically losers, but that’s the nature of the game," he said. "We take flat fees."

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Murdaugh, once a powerful Lowcountry lawyer from a prominent legal dynasty, was convicted in March 2023 of gunning down his wife and son, Maggie and Paul. Prosecutors argued at trial that Murdaugh killed them to gain sympathy and distract from a collapsing web of financial crimes, stolen client money and lies.

The case was thrown back into turmoil after allegations surfaced that court clerk Rebecca Hill made improper comments to jurors during the trial. Murdaugh’s defense argued she tampered with the jury to secure a guilty verdict and boost publicity for a book.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

The court found that Hill was drawn by the "siren call of celebrity" and allowed public attention to overcome her duty. The state has maintained that Murdaugh received a fair trial, but the South Carolina Supreme Court reversed the murder and weapons convictions and remanded the case for new proceedings.

Murdaugh’s attorneys have already previewed some of the issues they want to raise before any retrial, including a motion to test unknown male DNA reportedly found under Maggie’s fingernails.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

Fox News Digital has reached out to Murdaugh's attorneys and state prosecutors for comment.

item.title: Dem supported 'Green New Deal' after receiving nearly $60k from energy groups, now wants Congressional seat
item.description: Democrat Sean McCann's energy contributions from DTE Energy resurface as he campaigns to unseat Rep. Bill Huizenga in Michigan's 4th district.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: Sean McCann, a Democratic candidate for Congress in Michigan’s 4th Congressional District, has taken at least $57,600 in contributions from energy groups while supporting Green New Deal policies that were projected to increase utility costs, records show.

McCann’s record on energy is resurfacing as he wages an uphill campaign to unseat Rep. Bill Huizenga, R-Mich., and raises questions about his approach to federal energy policy. McCann first served as the Kalamazoo city commissioner from 1999 to 2008. He would then go on to serve in the Michigan House from 2011 to 2015 and in the Michigan Senate from 2019 to 2023. During his time in the Senate, he served as chair of the Senate Energy and Environment Committee.

Since 2011, records show that McCann has accepted at least $57,600 from the DTE Energy PAC, a Michigan-based energy group, and CMS Energy Corporation Employees for Better Government. He also accepted at least $560 in food and beverages from energy lobbyists since 2021.

DTE, in particular, provides energy for millions of customers in the state and describes itself as a politically active organization.

DAVID MARCUS: IN BOOMING DETROIT, VOTERS SKEPTICAL OF SOCIALISM SNAKE OIL

"DTE Energy is an active participant in the political process and encourages its employees to participate in the process. DTE Energy supports public policies that promote its commitment to sustainable, reliable, affordable and safe energy," the group states on its website.

Notably, according to FEC records, the group has donated to candidates on both sides of the aisle, including Rep. Bob Latta, R-Ohio, a top Republican on the House Energy and Commerce Committee.

McCann has also supported policies projected to increase utility costs, such as SB 271 of 2023 — Michigan’s "Green New Deal," as Republican critics called it.

The bill, which ultimately passed and was signed into law by Gov. Gretchen Whitmer in 2023, set clean energy requirements in order to reach a 100% clean energy standard by 2040.

EUROPE'S 'GREEN IDEOLOGY' CLIMATE AGENDA RISKS CRUSHING ITS INDUSTRY, ENVIRONMENT MINISTER WARNS

Among its many provisions, it required utility companies to implement at least a 50% renewable energy portfolio by 2030, a change some experts project will spike energy costs for consumers.

"That plan would cost Michiganders $386 billion by 2050, which amounts to an average annual cost increase of $2,746 per customer, or an additional $228.83 on monthly utility rates," the center said in a report on the plan.

"It would expose Michigan residents to a greater risk of blackouts. The most prolonged blackout based on the model would last 61 continuous hours during winter when Michigan residents need reliable energy the most," the report added.

The Mackinac Center is a policy nonprofit that champions the principles of free markets and limited government.

DTE did not respond to requests for comment on whether the company had taken a position on the plan or whether it stood to benefit from its implementation.

McCann praised the bill’s passage as a key environmental piece of legislation.

"These policies will safeguard public health and better protect our environment now and for future generations," McCann said in a statement following its passage.

During that same time, McCann voted against an amendment for a separate bill, SB 502, that would have prevented utility companies from raising electric rates on residential Michigan customers until 2040.

When asked about the contributions and his voting record, McCann’s campaign noted that his opponent, Huizenga, has also taken considerable amounts from DTE.

DEM ‘EPSTEIN CLASS’ ATTACK BACKFIRES AS TOP CANDIDATES FUNDED BY EPSTEIN-TIED BILLIONAIRE

"Bill Huizenga cashed more than $250,000 from utility companies and then voted to jack up our energy prices – and like the typical politician Huizenga is, he’s now trying to cover up his own corrupt record by lying about Sean," Simon Archer-Krauss, a campaign spokesperson, said in a statement.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Huizenga has received 42 payments from DTE, totaling $80,500 since 2010, according to FEC records. DTE has also contributed to his joint leadership fund and to his PAC to the tune of $6,000 and $22,500, respectively.

McCann has yet to clinch the Democratic nomination to officially challenge Huizenga in the November general election.

Michigan will hold its primary elections on Aug. 4.

item.title: NHL's top American-born scorer Patrick Kane signs deal to return to Chicago Blackhawks
item.description: Patrick Kane returns to the Chicago Blackhawks on a two-year, $16 million deal after posting 57 points with Detroit last season in 67 games.
item.content:encoded:
Considering it will no doubt be hanging in the United Center rafters at some point, I think most people assumed that the last time a Chicago Blackhawks No. 88 sweater would hit the ice was in Patrick Kane's final game before getting traded to the New York Rangers in 2023.

Wrong.

On Thursday, the Blackhawks announced that Kane, who won three Stanley Cups in Chicago and is the top American-born scorer in NHL history, is returning to the team on a two-year, $16 million deal.

CONNOR BEDARD RE-SIGNS WITH BLACKHAWKS, ENDING HOPES FOR ANOTHER NHL OFFER SHEET THIS OFFSEASON

It's a great deal for Kane. According to Puckpedia, his previous contracts with Detroit were all one-year deals, the biggest being worth $4 million. His new AAV is double that.

Of course, Chicago, being a younger team, has the cap flexibility to overpay a little bit, but there's a good chance this will be worth every cent.

For starters, Kane had a solid year last year, posting 57 points (16G, 41A) in 67 games. Is it on par with the production at the pinnacle of his career? No, but for a Blackhawks team that needs scoring, it's a huge help, especially when it comes to setting up scoring opportunities.

He'll also be key for the Connor Bedard-less Blackhawks early in the season because if they want a chance of sneaking into the postseason, they'll have to rack up some points while No. 98 is sidelined.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

The Blackhawks could be a tough team to play at home, especially early in the year. Imagine how electric the United Center will be for Kane's return. Chicago fans haven't had much to cheer about over the last few years, so maybe that excitement will be something the team can feed off of.

At 37 years old, there's a good chance this is Kane's final NHL contract.

And, if it is, what better way to wrap things up than by returning to the team he helped put back on the map in the 2010s.

Maybe he can do it again in the 2020s.

item.title: Leavitt rips blue state’s explosive noncitizen voter-roll admission: ‘Not a coincidence’
item.description: Karoline Leavitt says New Jersey's admission of 6,600 noncitizens on voter rolls is no coincidence as Trump renews his SAVE America Act push.
item.content:encoded:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt slammed New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill over noncitizens registered to vote in the Garden State, questioning the timing of the revelation as Trump tightens election security.

"I will say on New Jersey and this governor, the fact that she just all of a sudden decided to put out a press release saying President Trump and Republicans have been right about non-citizens on our voting rolls is not a coincidence," said Leavitt.

"I think this governor is saying this because she realizes this administration is serious about getting to the bottom of illegal aliens voting and being on our voter rolls in our country," she added.

NEW JERSEY REMOVES 6,600 PEOPLE FROM VOTER ROLLS WHO WERE ELIGIBLE TO CAST A BALLOT FOR 2024 ELECTION

Sherrill announced Tuesday that 6,600 noncitizens were registered to vote through New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle System due to a "serious software error," which the company that provides identity-related security services denied any wrongdoing. Leavitt says New Jersey's acknowledgment of noncitizens on voter rolls further validates the administration's election integrity concerns.

Sherrill "is speaking truth about the fact that this does take place in states across the country. It is completely unacceptable to have non-citizens on American voter rolls voting in U.S. elections. and she says it's 6,600," said Leavitt. "We suspect it's a heck of a lot more, not just in New Jersey, but in states across the country."

The revelation of noncitizens on New Jersey voter rolls comes less than a week after Trump‘s primetime address to the nation which he called for securing "free and fair" elections.

TRUMP INTERVENES AS HOUSE GOP REVOLT PUTS SAVE AMERICA ACT AT CENTER OF SPENDING SHOWDOWN

Trump again highlighted the issue of noncitizens on voter rolls as he renewed his push for the SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship and photo identification to register and vote in federal elections.

"I've also ordered DHS to notify every state about noncitizens on their voter rolls and direct them to remove all ineligible voters from the lists immediately," said Trump at the time.

Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin held a press conference following Trump’s election remarks and warned that election officials who disregard information provided by DHS could face fines, penalties or even prison time.

CALIFORNIA ACCUSED OF BLOCKING FEDERAL VOTER ROLL AUDIT AS DOJ ESCALATES PROBE OF ELECTION FRAUD CLAIMS

When asked by a reporter if anyone involved in the latest revelation in New Jersey, Leavitt referred the reporter to the Department of Justice.

"If the election officials, once we gave them the information they need to secure their elections and they chose not to, then those individuals can also be held accountable by fines, by penalties, and even depending on how far it goes, prison time," said Mullin.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Office of Gov. Sherrill for comment.

item.title: Trump to unveil massive coalition protecting Americans from rising costs as admin pursues a top priority
item.description: Trump expands the Ratepayer Protection Pledge with 23 governors and 200 stakeholders vowing data centers won't raise electricity costs for families.
item.content:encoded:
President Donald Trump is set to make a major Thursday announcement about safeguarding Americans from a common concern that stems from burgeoning data centers nationwide.

The 47th president is expected to announce at the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) headquarters in Washington, D.C. a coalition of legislators, tech leaders and utility CEOs who together have pledged not to pass along utility costs to customers as data centers continue to be erected nationwide, Fox News Digital has confirmed. The coalition is called the Ratepayer Protection Protection Pledge.

The coalition includes 23 governors — all Republicans. Four of them will be present Thursday.

Georgia Gov. Brian Kemp, Nebraska Gov. Jim Pillen, Idaho Gov. Brad Little and Louisiana Gov. Jeff Landry are scheduled to attend the event with the president. Trump is expected to make the announcement alongside Energy Secretary Chris Wright and EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin.

FOX NEWS POLL: MOVE OVER BIG BROTHER, VOTERS SEE BIG TECH AS GREATER THREAT TO US

Some Americans are skeptical of data centers, and for a variety of different reasons.

One of those being the potential for skyrocketing energy costs stemming from the massive facilities, which are largely being built by major tech companies to power the growing artificial intelligence sector.

Data centers house computer servers, data storage systems and networking equipment like routers and switches. They require large amounts of electricity and water, especially to power their own cooling systems.

THE REAL REASON YOUR ELECTRIC BILL IS SOARING THIS SUMMER WILL SURPRISE YOU

But on the energy cost front, Trump in February promised that rising prices would not be passed along to consumers.

Trump signed the Ratepayer Protection Pledge alongside those tech companies, including Microsoft, Google, Amazon, OpenAI, xAI and Oracle, in March.

Since then, demand to join the coalition has exploded among governors, utility CEOs and data center development companies, who will also sign onto the president’s pledge. A White House official said 200 stakeholders have signed the pledge.

"President Trump is expanding the Ratepayer Protection Pledge to governors, legislators, developers, and power providers to ensure everyone involved in building and powering data centers covers their own costs instead of passing them on to American families," White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers told Fox News Digital.

"The President’s bold action is turning data centers into engines of growth for local communities, while cementing America’s dominance in the global AI race."

THE HIDDEN LEFT-WING NETWORK POWERING A ‘CONSERVATIVE’ REBELLION AGAINST A MAJOR TRUMP PRIORITY

The rollout of data centers is a critical Trump administration economic priority, but rollouts of new centers have been subject to protests nationwide.

Fox News Digital recently reported that a group masquerading as a conservative grassroots organization has been teaming up with far-left activists to counter Trump's push.

In Virginia, a well-known Marxist leader from the Party of Socialism and Liberation (PSL) has become the face of the anti-data center movement.

item.title: Former ESPN star Trey Wingo claps back at Emmanuel Acho over his claim about Ryan Clark building 'NFL Live'
item.description: Trey Wingo fired back at Emmanuel Acho with ESPN ratings data after Acho claimed Ryan Clark built "NFL Live" into a juggernaut on the network.
item.content:encoded:
Former ESPN star Trey Wingo wants to set the record straight.

Former NFL linebacker and ex-ESPN analyst Emmanuel Acho claimed that Ryan Clark built ESPN’s "NFL Live" into the "juggernaut" it is today, and Wingo was not having any of it.

"Ryan Clark built NFL Live into the juggernaut it is today (along with Rutledge, Mina, and Spears), he didn’t ideate NFL Live, there’s a difference," Acho posted.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Wingo, who hosted "NFL Live" for 15 years, from 2003 to 2018, clapped back at Acho’s claim that Clark built the show by posting a graphic showing its ratings over the years, which he said disproved Acho’s claim.

The graphic, which was based on numbers that ESPN reported, showed that the "NFL Live" averaged 688,000 viewers during a comparable early-season period in 2013 and 624,000 in 2012. It averaged roughly 486,000 in 2015, 492,000 in 2016, 449,000 in 2021 and 440,000 in 2025.

Wingo captioned his post on X, "’Let your apology be as loud as your disrespect.’ I think someone said that once."

The former ESPN host’s line about letting your apology be as loud as your disrespect referenced a spat in which Acho insulted former San Francisco 49ers wide receiver Deebo Samuel and later apologized to him.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON'T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

Acho's initial tweet was in response to OutKick’s Dan Zaksheske. Zaksheske began the thread of interaction by responding to a clip from "The Breakfast Club," where the morning show hosts said Clark built "NFL Live."

"NFL Live premiered in 1998 when Clark was 18 years old, four years before he entered the NFL and 17 years before he joined ESPN," Zaksheske wrote on X.

Acho responded to Zaksheske and agreed with "The Breakfast Club," saying "And nobody was watching NFL Live then," referencing the show’s existence before Clark joined it.

Wingo spent nearly 23 years at ESPN and was not retained in November 2020.

A large part of his tenure was spent hosting and building "NFL Live," and was the host during its peak year in 2013.

Clark joined ESPN in 2015 and spent more than 11 seasons with the network, overlapping for about five years with Wingo. As his time at ESPN progressed, he became an increasingly polarizing figure.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Fox News Digital reached out to Acho for comment but did not immediately get a response.

OutKick's Dan Zaksheske contributed to this report.

Follow Fox News Digital’s sports coverage on X, and subscribe to the Fox News Sports Huddle newsletter.

item.title: New CIA thriller 'The Fix' blends heist, espionage genres with Liam Neeson, Zachary Levi
item.description: The trailer for "The Fix" shows Liam Neeson and Zachary Levi as CIA operators pulling off a daring heist deep inside Tehran after Afghanistan goes wrong.
item.content:encoded:
The trailer has arrived for Liam Neeson's upcoming CIA film, "The Fix."

Basic info:

HOLLYWOOD LEGEND MAKING MOVIE ABOUT EPIC IRAN RESCUE MISSION TO SAVE F-15 FIGHTER JET CREW

There's nothing that gets the blood pumping like a great CIA film. The world of black ops secrets and espionage is such a fun genre.

Well, enter "The Fix."

A team of CIA commandos find themselves hunting money deep inside Iran. Pretty timely, given the massive war that is currently raging against the regime.

The trailer paints a picture that it's going to be the perfect guy film. Give it a watch below, and let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

This movie looks like it's going to be legit, and it's not often you get a spy film involving Iran. Add in the fact the cast is being led by Liam Neeson with Zachary Levi playing a major role, and all the ingredients are there for a very fun film.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

It also blends the heist genre with the espionage genre. Again, it's almost like it was built in a lab for your average American male viewer.

That's not a complaint. That's a compliment.

You can catch "The Fix" starting Sept. 11, and make sure to let me know your thoughts at David.Hookstead@outkick.com.

item.title: Michigan Democrat accuses Abdul El-Sayed of 'deeply sexist attacks' on female political rivals
item.description: Rep. Hillary Scholten accuses Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making "sexist attacks" against Rep. Haley Stevens and Gov. Gretchen Whitmer.
item.content:encoded:
Rep. Hillary Scholten, D-Mich., accused progressive Democratic Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed of making "deeply sexist attacks" against female opponents during a Wednesday interview, citing his criticism of Rep. Haley Stevens, D-Mich., and Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer as voters prepare for the state's Aug. 4 primary.

"There is a uniquely sexist bent to the things that he has said about women," Scholten told anchor Jake Tapper on CNN's "The Lead." "He said that Congresswoman Stevens couldn’t string two sentences together, that, you know, she was sort of an empty vessel."

Scholten, who endorsed Stevens in the race to replace retiring Sen. Gary Peters, D-Mich., argued El-Sayed had disparaged Stevens’ intelligence and personality rather than focusing on policy disagreements.

DAVID MARCUS: IN BOOMING DETROIT, VOTERS SKEPTICAL OF SOCIALISM SNAKE OIL

"It’s one thing to attack your opponent because of the substance of their issues," Scholten said. "It’s another thing to engage in what are deeply sexist attacks."

Fox News Digital reached out to Stevens and El-Sayed for comment but did not immediately receive a response.

El-Sayed previously told Semafor that Stevens was "a suit with a large AIPAC account" and suggested the pro-Israel group might teach her "how to string together two coherent sentences."

DEM CIVIL WAR HITS PRIMARY DEBATE STAGE IN FIERY BATTLEGROUND SHOWDOWN: 'WHAT ARE YOU HIDING?'

Scholten pointed to El-Sayed's rhetoric during his unsuccessful 2018 gubernatorial campaign against Whitmer, whose father previously served as president and CEO of Blue Cross Blue Shield of Michigan.

"Going back to his race with Governor Whitmer, he said she was bought and sold for by her dad’s company," Scholten said. "Essentially comparing her to property with no ideas of her own."

El-Sayed spokesperson Roxie Richner pushed back when Scholten first publicly raised concerns about his record with women.

"Unless the congresswoman has specific accusations she would like to make, perhaps it’s better to avoid repeating throwaway talking points to push an unfounded narrative," Richner said to Semafor. 

ABDUL EL-SAYED SAYS RALLYING WITH HASAN PIKER DOESN'T MEAN HE ENDORSES 'AMERICA DESERVED 9/11' REMARK

He addressed his remarks about Stevens during a Sunday interview on CNN's "State of the Union" after Tapper confronted him with the "two coherent sentences" comment.

"I don’t think it’s kind to make fun of somebody for how they talk or how they show up," El-Sayed said. "I also don’t think it’s kind to make insinuations about somebody’s character, as is being done to the tune of tens of millions of dollars."

Scholten said she was even more concerned by El-Sayed's decision to campaign with left-wing streamer Hasan Piker, who said in 2019 that "America deserved 9/11." El-Sayed has defended appearing with Piker while distancing himself from the comment.

"I give him credit for disavowing it after the fact, but the reality is he also didn’t have to choose to campaign with Hasan Piker," Scholten said. "To me, that’s a nonstarter."

When asked if she would support El-Sayed if he defeated Stevens, Scholten did not provide a direct answer to CNN.

"I cannot, I will not even come close to being associated with comments like America deserved September 11," Scholten said.

The congresswoman warned that embracing candidates associated with such rhetoric could further damage the Democratic Party.

"I think that condoning behavior like this from candidates like Abdul El-Sayed is taking us in a direction that we don’t want to go as a party," Scholten said.

El-Sayed's campaign urged supporters Friday to stop making personal attacks against Stevens.

"It’s become impossible to ignore the way that people online make fun of my opponent for things that have nothing to do with her policies or politics. It’s unkind and unhelpful," El-Sayed said. "If you support me, please stop."

item.title: Johnson delivers big for Trump, but frustration grows across razor-thin GOP majority
item.description: Frustrations mount over how Speaker Mike Johnson manages the razor-thin House majority even as the GOP nets a series of big wins.
item.content:encoded:
Speaker Mike Johnson, R-La., enjoyed a series of wins this week – but there’s little time to celebrate, as Republicans grow increasingly frustrated with how he is managing the razor-thin House majority.

"He’s capitulating to the wants of the Freedom Caucus," one House Republican, granted anonymity to speak freely, told Fox News Digital. "The Freedom Caucus are in control. The rest of us are just following."

The Louisiana Republican is fresh off a slew of legislative victories this week, notching success with bills curbing congressional stock trading, averting a government shutdown, and setting the stage for a $95 billion budget that includes a top election priority of President Donald Trump.

GOP'S $95B DEFENSE PACKAGE SURVIVES CRITICAL HURDLE AMID FISCAL HAWK REVOLT

Johnson has managed to snatch victory from the jaws of defeat dozens of times since winning the speaker’s gavel in October 2023. But the pressure of running a razor-thin House majority is still enough to send tensions boiling, particularly within an ideologically diverse Republican conference before what’s expected to be a politically bruising battle to keep control of Congress.

Several House Republicans have told Fox News Digital this week that Johnson is not doing enough to bolster his rank-and-file members and their own individual legislative priorities.

A second House GOP lawmaker granted anonymity to speak candidly, said, "there’s frustration within the conference around the speaker not pushing back, and not saying no to the White House."

'IT'S A MESS': GOP TURNS ON HOUSE CONSERVATIVES AS VOTER ID BLOCKADE STALLS TRUMP'S AGENDA

"Members have said ‘We don’t report to the White House. We don’t report to the executive branch. We should be able to make these decisions on our own,’" the second House GOP lawmaker said.

Johnson’s allies have consistently argued that his close relationship with Trump has been key to his staying power and success.

But not everyone believes Johnson is prioritizing the needs of his conservative right flank or the president over others.

TOP TRUMP OFFICIAL MAKES NEW SAVE ACT CASE TO REPUBLICANS BEHIND CLOSED DOORS

Rep. Keith Self, R-Texas, a member of the House Freedom Caucus, told Fox News Digital, "There does seem to be a focus on a few in the conference, and the reasoning is, the most vulnerable. But that by definition excludes some other members."

"All I will say is, we need to continue to focus on what our base wants, because we have a very important election coming up here pretty quick," Self said.

Others, still, simply believe House GOP leadership could be doing more to help lawmakers across the conference.

"I know it’s hard. I mean, being the speaker is the hardest job in the country," Rep. Nicole Malliotakis, R-N.Y., told Fox News Digital on Tuesday.

And while she maintained that Johnson was still very much in control of the House floor and his fellow Republicans, she said, "I think there’s more that can be done to help members score wins that may be small in general, but are big and important for their districts."

"I think they haven’t been proactive in going to the membership and saying, ‘Give me your top three priorities, not controversial, don’t cost money,’ and help us navigate the committee process to get those to the floor," Malliotakis said. "I’ve got a bill for example, like, [high-occupancy vehicle] lanes for veterans — allowing veterans who are going to the VA, or going for treatment, to be able to use the HOV lanes…I’ve got one that would…go after heroin injection centers in New York."

Rep. Tim Burchett, R-Tenn., was even more blunt: "You’ve got to kiss a– to move legislation up here, and it’s just, it’s really getting to be a ridiculous situation."

The frustrations hitting Johnson from different sides of the conference are a consequence of Republicans’ razor-thin House majority — only being able to lose two seats on any party-line vote with full attendance — and the vastly different approaches to legislating.

Still, other House Republicans maintain that Johnson is doing a good job of sticking by his allies.

"The ones who are complaining today will be the same ones who are complaining even when times are good. It’s just who they are," a third House Republican, speaking anonymously, told Fox News Digital.

And Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-S.D., said House Republicans have managed to accomplish a significant number of their policy goals this term.

"It’s actually a pretty remarkable testament both to the patience of Mike Johnson and to the willingness of the White House to engage," the South Dakota Republican said.

Another Johnson ally who was granted anonymity pointed out that he spends his non-legislative time raising money for campaigns across the conference, whether people agree with him or not. "Outside the Capitol, Johnson has a second full-time job as lead fundraiser — he’s on track to raise $500 million to ensure everyone, no matter their caucus, returns next year in the majority."

Johnson’s allies would also argue that the broad range of complaints highlighted in this story is an example of his focus on trying to listen to every member of the House GOP, and that there has not been any push for his ouster despite the rumblings.

But with such a slim House majority, it’s a fact that every vote counts.

As the second House Republican put it, "Everyone has a bullet in the chamber, and they’re not afraid to use it."

item.title: Republicans fall in line with Trump on Iran war despite rising costs and US death toll
item.description: Sen. Bill Cassidy proved the decisive vote again as Senate Republicans blocked Democrats' war powers resolution to restrict Trump's Iran strikes.
item.content:encoded:
Senate Republicans, despite a mounting death toll, surging energy prices and growing uncertainty, batted down another attempt by Democrats to handcuff President Donald Trump in his war with Iran.

Democrats pulled the trigger on yet another war powers resolution, the first since Trump resumed strikes in the Middle East, and were again unsuccessful in convincing enough Republicans to force the administration to get authorization from Congress.

Thursday’s war powers resolution was forced by Sen. Chris Van Hollen, D-Md., who argued that, "the main thing is that we need to continue to hold senators of both parties accountable for ending this senseless war."

REPUBLICANS BREAK WITH TRUMP TO REBUKE IRAN WAR — BUT IT WON'T CHANGE POLICY

"You know, President Trump said that we won the war back in March," Van Hollen said. "And the question we've got is: If we won the war in March, how is it that American service members are still being killed and gas prices are going through the roof again?"

Van Hollen, and nearly every single Senate Democrat — save for Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pa., who has routinely voted against war powers resolutions — only managed to carve off one Republican, Sen. Susan Collins, R-Maine, in their pursuit of reining in Trump. 

Sens. Lisa Murkowski, R-Alaska, and Rand Paul, R-Ky., who have routinely voted with Democrats on war powers, did not vote.

SENATE DEMOCRATS FINALLY CRACK GOP UNITY ON TRUMP'S IRAN WAR AS MURKOWSKI FLIPS

Whether the resolution would be successful remained up in the air until moments before the vote.

Sen. Bill Cassidy, R-La., who previously acted as the decisive vote to advance a war powers resolution last month, was again the key wild card on Thursday. But just before the alarm sounded to open the vote in the Senate, Cassidy revealed he would oppose Van Hollen’s effort.

"I am going to oppose the War Powers resolution," Cassidy said. "I think it's impractical to, like, withdraw tomorrow."

The Senate’s failed effort comes after Democrats in the House scored a win against Trump, where they carved off just enough Republican support to pass their own war powers resolution.

4 HOUSE REPUBLICANS REBEL AGAINST TRUMP, VOTE WITH DEMS TO BLOCK IRAN WAR POWERS

In arguing against the move, Sen. Tim Sheehy, R-Mont., urged Senate Democrats to recognize the dangers that Iran posed to the U.S., and to the service members overseas.

"They want all of us dead. They don't want us to disappear with the snap of a finger," Sheehy said on the Senate floor. "They want us to be raped, burned alive, and cut into pieces. Watch the video from October 7th."

"That's what they want to happen," he continued. "From Maine to San Diego, from Seattle to Miami, they want this country turned into a burning husk of its former self."

item.title: Ethiopian migrant allegedly killed 3 over $65 parking dispute, claimed he was victim of anti-immigrant bias
item.description: Tsegaab Ademassu Binessu allegedly killed three St. Paul apartment employees over a $65 parking contract before fleeing hundreds of miles north.
item.content:encoded:
An Ethiopian migrant allegedly murdered three employees at his St. Paul apartment building after becoming enraged over a $65 parking deck contract, days after Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz was rebuked for defending the pardon of an illegal immigrant sex offender.

Police recovered nearly three dozen 9mm shell casings from the management office after Tsegaab Ademassu Binessu, 30, allegedly went on a rampage over apartment parking contract policies. Authorities said he later complained about the U.S. immigration system.

Binessu, who the local FOX affiliate said had been employed as a truck driver until recently, became angry after trying to cancel his parking contract shortly after renewing it because he was considering joining the U.S. Navy.

TIM WALZ PARDONS ILLEGAL ALIEN CONVICTED OF ARMED ROBBERY BEFORE ICE DEPORTATION TO LAOS

Adam "AJ" Wilwerding, 43, Nansi Fuentes Zambrano, 25, and Dena Silcox, 56, were found dead Monday after Binessu allegedly told an employee who answered the phone that he was coming to the office, according to the New York Post.

Wilwerding and Silcox were shot six times each, while Zambrano was reportedly shot as she tried to escape through a back-office window.

MAN CHARGED IN CRASH THAT KILLED MOTHER AND TWO YOUNG SONS HAS ICE DETAINER, PRIOR DWI CONVICTIONS

Binessu allegedly confessed during a phone call with his longtime roommate, who said the migrant had "argued with apartment management and ended up killing three people," according to the local FOX affiliate.

Binessu was eventually arrested in Virginia, Minnesota, several hundred miles north of St. Paul near International Falls and the Canadian border.

PATEL TURNS TABLES ON WALZ IN RESPONSE TO VIRAL TWEET ON MINNESOTA FRAUD RAIDS: 'COME AGAIN?'

He was detained while driving one of the victims' cars and reportedly complained to investigators about the American political system and his inability to bring his wife and children from Ethiopia to the United States, which the local FOX affiliate said contributed to his divorce.

Binessu also reportedly complained he was being treated differently because of his race and immigration status.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Fox News Radio host Guy Benson noted how Binessu had believed he was unfairly treated because "he is Black and an immigrant."

"So, he allegedly shot three women to death," Benson said. "Will Gov. Walz warn us not to judge this man over his ‘worst day,’ or whatever his recent excuse was for pardoning & defending that child sexual abuser?"

Fox News Digital reached out to Walz for a response.

item.title: Yankees player's pitch clock antics prompt wild hot-mic moment, backlash: 'You're picking on me'
item.description: Hot mic audio captured Jose Caballero telling an umpire he's being "picked on" after a pitch-clock violation, while Aaron Boone calls the ruling "embarrassing."
item.content:encoded:
New York Yankees infielder Jose Caballero's pitch-clock maneuvers irked multiple parties earlier this week.

On Monday night, the benches cleared against the Pittsburgh Pirates after pitcher Dennis Santana had some choice words for Caballero, and two days later, hot mics caught Caballero in a heated discussion with an umpire.

In short, Caballero has become notorious for waiting until the last possible second to make eye-contact with the pitcher, which, in the pitch-clock era, is required before a pitcher comes set or starts his windup. Batters and pitchers have until there are eight seconds left to become alert with the opponent.

Caballero did his usual on Wednesday afternoon, though, and was promptly called for a violation before striking out, despite even looking up a second earlier. Mics picked up Caballero saying that the umpire was "picking on" him. Home plate umpire Quinn Wolcott said Caballero did not "get to do the three-four seconds thing in the box anymore."

Caballero replied that he had been "doing my f—ing routine," and manager Aaron Boone said the umps were "making up rules as we go."

Boone said that neither he nor anyone else on the Yankees were made aware that Caballero could potentially be called for violations. However, Wolcott cited the MLB rules, which state that maneuvers by batters "to deceive a pitcher into beginning their windup or coming to the set position" will first result in warnings before being called for pitch-clock violations.

Crew chief Adrian Johnson said Caballero has "been warned all season," prompting a change in how he is dealt with.

FATHER TURNING FAMILY TRAGEDY INTO EVERY BASEBALL FAN'S DREAM OF VISITING 30 MLB STADIUMS IN 30 DAYS

"He's been warned all season. We were instructed not to warn him. We were instructed to violate him. That's what it is. It's a violation," he said to a pool reporter.

So, as long as umps believe Caballero is purposely baiting pitchers, the Yankees might have a losing battle here.

Johnson also said that the violation was a result of the benches-clearing incident on Monday.

"He’s not allowed to do an inordinate amount of time looking down, trying to get the pitcher to cause a violation," Wolcott told a pool reporter. "There’s no other hitter that gets in the box, takes the amount of the time that Caballero does while looking down, not making eye contact with the pitcher. Obviously it’s caused a lot of problems in the past. It’s not going to be allowed anymore."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

Boone called the umpires' decisions "embarrassing." Based on previous conversations with MLB, Boone instructed Caballero to "make sure you’re looking up at nine so that you are alert by eight."

"He actually said that they were making new rules today and it’s on me because I was deceiving or something like that to the pitcher, which doesn’t make sense," Caballero said. "I don’t know, maybe my English is so bad that I don’t understand, but deceiving kind of means that you’re trying to trick someone and I’m not trying to trick someone because I do the same thing over and over. You can check my last 50 at-bats and see if I do it the same or not, or check maybe 100 at-bats or 150."

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

item.title: Florida woman on the run and wanted for murder relied on network of alleged accomplices to evade capture: Cops
item.description: Isabelle Rose Johnson allegedly uses disguises and aliases to evade arrest after Jason Christopher Coulthart's remains were found in Freeport, Florida.
item.content:encoded:
A woman wanted in a murder case has spent weeks slipping through law enforcement's grasp, with investigators alleging a string of suspected accomplices helped keep her hidden while she remains on the run.

Isabelle Rose Johnson, 38, is wanted on charges in the killing of Jason Christopher Coulthart. A warrant for her arrest was issued on June 26, and she has been dodging detectives ever since.

The Walton County Sheriff's Office said Johnson, who is a "cunning master manipulator," has been relying on the help of others to stay incognito. Five people have been arrested in connection to the murder investigation.

FLORIDA WOMAN CHARGED WITH MURDER EVADING POLICE AFTER BODY FOUND IN SHALLOW GRAVE: SHERIFF

As the sheriff's office continues to hunt Johnson down, investigators shared different photos of Johnson seen on surveillance and a mugshot from Carroll County, Georgia.

SEND US A TIP

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

Johnson has worn different hairstyles, hats and even eyeglasses to change up her appearance.

FOLLOW US ON X

She also has reportedly been giving out different names to identify herself, like "Harley, Jessica Elaine Bowman, and Denise" to different people she comes in contact with, the sheriff's office said.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

As for the man she is accused of killing, Coulthart's remains were found on June 25, buried on a property in Freeport, located in Florida's Panhandle.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE U.S. NEWS

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE

He was reported missing a month earlier on May 24. The last time Coulthart was seen alive was when he was leaving the College Condominiums on East College Boulevard.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

The 43-year-old victim leaves behind a son, according to his obituary.

GET BREAKING NEWS BY EMAIL

Sheriff Michael Adkinson said, "Anyone found to have helped or is still assisting Isabelle Johnson avoid arrest will be charged accordingly."

GET BREAKING NEWS ALERTS

He went on to say, "If you give her a place to stay, help her hide, or lie for her, you will face consequences. This is a homicide case. We will not allow anyone to get in the way of this investigation."

Multiple law enforcement organizations have come together to offer up a $16,000 reward for information that could lead to Johnson's arrest.

item.title: Men switched at birth uncover hospital mix-up after DNA test decades later
item.description: A DNA test gift revealed Kyle Bylin had no genetic match to his family, leading to the discovery he and Jeremy Morrison were switched at birth in 1988.
item.content:encoded:
Two North Dakota men are suing a local hospital after discovering they were switched at birth nearly four decades ago, a revelation brought to light by a surprise result from a direct-to-consumer genetic test.

The men joined Fox & Friends on Thursday to discuss their journey.

The mix-up was uncovered two years ago when Kyle Bylin received an at-home DNA test as a gift. Bylin says he was shocked when the results showed no genetic match to anyone in the family that raised him.

TWO FAMILIES SUE NORTH DAKOTA HOSPITAL ON ALLEGATIONS BABIES WERE SWITCHED AT BIRTH

"It was absolutely mind-blowing," Bylin told Fox & Friends. "We knew the DNA trail didn’t make sense, but to find out you’ve been switched at birth is a huge revelation."

Instead, the data pointed to a biological aunt, leading Bylin to her nephew, Jeremy Morrison. Subsequent DNA testing confirmed that Morrison and Bylin had been raised by each other’s biological families.

"[My parents] will always be my parents. A DNA test isn’t going to change 38 years of memories overnight," Morrison said.

Morrison recalled having long-standing doubts while growing up, noting that he often made light of the physical differences between himself and the woman who raised him.

MELINDA FRENCH GATES SAYS IT TOOK 3 DOCTORS TO DIAGNOSE THE CONDITION SHE WAS SUFFERING FROM

"I kind of had a feeling inside that I was adopted or, you know, she stole me or something like that," Morrison said. But that doesn’t necessarily mean he’d change what happened, he added.

"It’s a little bit of nature, a little bit of nurture, and you end up with the life that you have," he said when asked if he’d go back and undo the switch.

SISTERS IN VIRAL VIDEO REVEAL TRUTH OF 'EERIE' FAMILY PHOTO THEY DISCOVERED AT RENTAL HOME

The emotional toll of the discovery has weighed heavily on both men as they grapple with the realization that their upbringings were altered.

"Do I feel like I lost the ability to truly connect with my biological parents? Absolutely," Bylin said.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR HEALTH NEWSLETTER

The two men filed a lawsuit against Unity Medical Center alleging emotional distress caused by negligence and medical malpractice, claiming the facility robbed them of the lives they were meant to live.

Unity Medical Center denied responsibility for the incident, asserting that an internal review yielded no evidence showing hospital personnel were at fault. However, the medical center did not dispute that the swap occurred.

TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ

"We recognize the profound impact this discovery has had on them and their families," the hospital said in a statement.

"While we deeply sympathize with the men and their families, we have found no evidence to support claims that Unity Medical Center or its staff were responsible for what occurred."

Although both Bylin and Morrison have since met their respective biological parents, the two men have yet to meet each other in person.

"There are no plans in the works right now," Morrison said. "I grew up with his entire family. And he grew up with my entire family," he added, as the reason the two men "don’t feel like" they need to meet.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE HEALTH STORIES

Despite the legal battle and the lingering disruption to their lives, both men said they are focusing on moving forward and making the best of their circumstances.

"We’re just trying to raise awareness of this situation," Bylin added.

item.title: Candidate who wants to abolish the Senate, arrest Netanyahu offers DSA shot at swing-seat breakthrough
item.description: Oliver Larkin says he supports arresting Benjamin Netanyahu, abolishing the U.S. Senate, and ending military aid to Israel ahead of the Florida primary.
item.content:encoded:
EXCLUSIVE - Far-left Democratic congressional candidate Oliver Larkin of Florida says he supports abolishing the U.S. Senate, agrees with socialist New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested during his upcoming trip to the U.S., and refers to Israel's ruling coalition as a "religious supremacist regime."

Larkin, who is challenging Democratic Rep. Jared Moskowitz in next month's Florida primary, is one of just four congressional candidates endorsed by the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA).

The 33-year-old Larkin, a veteran of progressive champion Sen. Bernie Sanders' 2016 Democratic presidential campaign, is also the only DSA-endorsed House candidate running in one of the roughly two dozen key swing districts that will determine if Republicans hold onto their razor-thin majority in the midterm elections.

Moskowitz, who is running for re-election in Florida's newly redrawn 25th Congressional District, a Broward County-anchored seat in the southeastern corner of the red-leaning state, is heavily supported by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee (DCCC). He currently holds a polling and fundraising lead over Larkin.

MIDTERM BATTLE FOR THE HOUSE: CHECK OUT THE FOX NEWS POWER RANKINGS

The face-off between Larkin and Moskowitz comes in the wake of victories by far-left and socialist candidates over more moderate contenders in deep-blue districts last month's primaries in New York City and Colorado.

The ideological battle between the left-wing and the center-left establishment to reshape the Democratic Party now moves to statewide primary showdowns in Michigan, Wisconsin and Minnesota early next month.

Those contests are followed by Florida's primary, where the spotlight on the burgeoning Democratic Party civil war will shine on the Moskowitz-Larkin showdown.

Larkin, in an exclusive interview this week with Fox News, spelled out where he stands on key issues.

Mamdani, in a video released on Tuesday, said that the New York Police Department would not be able to carry out an arrest warrant on Netanyahu that was issued by the International Criminal Court, the world's top war-crimes court.

But Mamdani argued the Israeli leader was a "war criminal" and urged federal authorities to arrest him.

ESTABLISHMENT DEMOCRATS TURN FIRE ON SOCIALIST WING AS PARTY REVOLT FLARES

Asked about Netanyahu, Larkin said, "We need to enforce the law, and to the degree possible, I would support the enforcement by the New York City Police Department or any other law enforcement entity in the United States to enforce that ICC warrant."

Larkin, who backs an end to all U.S. military aid to Israel, said he supports a "one-state solution with equal and democratic rights for all people...I do not support a religious supremacist regime, whether it be in Israel or Saudi Arabia, or any other country."

A horrific Hamas sneak attack on Oct. 7, 2023, killed roughly 1,200 people in Israel. The continued Israeli response over the past two and a half years has resulted in more than 73,000 people being killed, according to health officials in the Palestinian territory. Israel's response to the Hamas attack has led to condemnation of the country from across the globe, including from longtime advocates for the Jewish state.

In a stunning move, nearly half of House Democrats voted this week to cut off aid to Israel, illustrating the seismic shift over the past couple of years in political support for America's longtime ally in the Middle East.

The DSA released new policy proposals this week that include scrapping the Senate and expanding the House of Representatives, which would upend the American system of government in place since 1789.

Larkin said he agrees with top DSA officials who call the Senate an "undemocratic institution."

MEET THE FAR LEFT INSURGENTS WAGING WAR ON THE DEMOCRATIC PARTY IN THE 2026 MIDTERMS

"I do support the abolition of the Senate," Larkin told Fox News. "I live in Broward County, Florida. We have more people in Broward County than 12 states in the country, but you do not see a dozen or two dozen senators representing Broward County in the U.S. Senate. In South Florida, we have more people in the Miami metropolitan area than over 30 states, but you don't see a two-thirds majority of South Floridians in the United States Senate because this is an institution that is mathematically undemocratic."

Larkin charged that the Senate "privileges land over people, and it originates from a compromise with slave-owning states when the Constitution was first passed. We don't need to overturn the constitution or throw the constitution out. The founders envisioned a process through constitutional amendments where it could be amended over and over again."

Larkin criticized Moskowitz, a two-term congressman and former state lawmaker and county commissioner who served as Florida Division of Emergency Management director under GOP Gov. Ron DeSantis, for voting in favor of the Laken Riley Act and for supporting President Donald Trump's DOGE initiative.

He slammed "milquetoast neoliberal corporate Democrats like Jared Moskowitz" that he claimed failed to push through policies that Democrats voted for when they elected former President Joe Biden to the White House and boosted the party to control of Congress in 2020.

Fox News reached out to Moskowitz's re-election campaign for comment, but didn't receive a response at the time this story posted.

Larkin, in his conversation with Fox News, also made it clear that if he wins next month's primary and the general election in November, he won't be supporting House Democratic Leader Rep. Hakeem Jeffries of New York as speaker if the Democrats win back the majority.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

"I'm not prepared to support Leader Jeffries for a Democratic House speaker. "I will be supporting a Democrat over any Republican, but I was deeply concerned by Leader Jeffries hesitancy and ultimate failure to endorse Zohran Mamdani when he became the Democratic nominee for the City of New York, the city that Hakeem Jeffries represents in the House of Representatives."

Jeffries did endorse Mamdani in late October, as early voting in New York City's mayoral election got underway.

The Democratic House leader's backing of Mamdani came after months of pressure and following a four-month standoff where Jeffries stayed neutral in the wake of Mamdani's blockbuster June Democratic primary victory.

item.title: Lane Kiffin goes off script: Hulk Hogan, Ole Miss and social media dominate SEC Media Days
item.description: Lane Kiffin's SEC Media Days appearance featured a Hulk Hogan documentary reference, his social media absence explanation, and a Ring camera spy story.
item.content:encoded:
TAMPA, FL - Lane Kiffin stepped off the escalator inside the Marriott hotel on Thursday morning with cameras waiting to capture his every move at SEC Media Days, certainly knowing that all eyes would be on him over at least the upcoming four hours.

Just after noon, the LSU coach was back on his private plane and headed home to Baton Rouge with the Tigers media relations department and three of his star players.

Pete Golding drops unfiltered truth on 'Ex' Lane Kiffin, and NCAA tampering: 'What have we created?'

Over the past seven months, the anticipation had been brewing for Kiffin's appearance representing LSU after leaving Ole Miss before the Rebels college football playoff run.

There had been questions about whether Lane would actually embrace some of the questions that you just knew were coming his way.

But, thanks to a 22-minute filibuster at the podium after his introduction from commissioner Greg Sankey, it was Kiffin that talked just enough to only have to field upwards of four questions from the media gathered in the grand ballroom.

And, if you haven’t seen him posting on social media since May, there's also an answer to that interesting move as well.

"Really it just started with my son, Knox, saying 'you're on social media all the time, I bet you can't get off'. If you've followed me, you know that betting me is a good way to do it," Kiffin proclaimed. "I said 'yea I can, just watch'. So, I just got off everything, don't even have the apps.

"It wasn’t a plan for very long, I just wanted to show him I could do it. I think when I look back, a lot of good things have come when I subtract things from my life."

Yes, I know that's going to be hard to believe for plenty of college football fans who were either entertained or agitated with Kiffin's vocal presence on social media. Coming off the Vanity Fair piece in which he made some interesting remarks about recruiting at Ole Miss, some wondered whether or not this was a mandate from the school itself, or his agent Jimmy Sexton.

"See, look at you Trey, already on your phone," Kiffin jokingly mentioned as he walked out of the room.

But, you just knew there would be some type of head-scratching references made during his time in Tampa, even if that did not come at the expense of Ole Miss, or on social media.

I just didn’t expect Kiffin to start discussing the Netflix special on Hulk Hogan.

There have been plenty of comments made about Kiffin's departure from Ole Miss, so that story doesn’t need to be rehashed in this setting.

But, his comments about diversity problems in Oxford being a detriment to Ole Miss recruiting have not gone silent into the night.

And, as much as Kiffin would have enjoyed not having to open that can of worms in late July, the question was coming as to whether he regretted those comments to Vanity Fair.

Florida coach brings laughs, no filter and Lane Kiffin comparison rebuttal to first SEC media days

But, in true Lane fashion, he found a way to catch everyone off-guard with an explanation about how watching a Hulk Hogan documentary opened his eyes about regret and death.

"I made a decision that I changed jobs, and as I get older and I go through things and losing both parents," Kiffin lamented. "But, you know, I watched the Hulk Hogan special. I realized something. I was, like: We're all going to die. And so some of the things that we put importance on, we worry about, and what this person says and that, really aren't going to matter.

"When I lost my dad I started thinking that. I really started thinking, when I grew up there were two people that I thought just wouldn't die... my dad and Hulk Hogan. Then I watched that special, and Hulk Hogan died too. Like, there was no way that Hulk Hogan was ever going to not wrestle and die, and he did."

Well, that quote certainly opened some eyes, and rightfully so. If there was one thing I was not expecting from the LSU coach on Thursday, it was certainly a reference to the controversial Hulk Hogan.

But, that's Lane Kiffin for you. So, is it actually surprising?

The athletic departments at both schools could sign a peace treaty on the SEC Network for the Sept. 19 game, and it still wouldn’t calm the tension between Rebel fans and the coach who tried to divert attention away from their college football playoff run.

Lane Kiffin can't stop talking about Ole Miss, even while explaining why he chose LSU over Florida

After Pete Golding stole the show on Wednesday with his authentic answers, and rant against tampering rules in college athletics, the attention shifted a bit towards Ole Miss.

While there is no denying that the collision course these two teams are on for the showdown in Oxford, it looks as though SEC commissioner Greg Sankey's words about focusing on your own school during spring meetings seem to have been taken to heart, for now.

And, the fun between both head coaches has not gone silent into the night either.

Turns out, when Golding bought Kiffin's house in Oxford, Lane still had access to the Ring security cameras at his old residence. So, when LSU's staff was keeping tabs on a recruiting trip for Ole Miss, Kiffin had a sense of calm about his demeanor.

"He can't be wherever it was at this recruit's house," Kiffin recalled telling his LSU staff. "He's on my couch, I just saw him walk in."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

Through all the vitriol between Lane and the Ole Miss community, maybe Pete Golding will let him stay in one of spare bedrooms before the September showdown in Oxford that we're all anticipating.

We are just under two months away from a game that will have the college football world focusing their attention on the State of Mississippi.

Maybe by then, Kiffin will be back on social media, selling the showdown like Hulk Hogan would have sold the NWO.

item.title: New details from Nancy Guthrie ransom demand revealed, 'Her life is in your hands': report
item.description: A former Arizona reporter read new excerpts from the purported ransom notes in the Nancy Guthrie kidnapping case on the "Crime Junkie" podcast.
item.content:encoded:
New portions of the first purported ransom note received in the Nancy Guthrie investigation have been made public after a former Arizona reporter read them on a major true crime podcast Wednesday.

Briana Whitney, who was working for the Phoenix-based AZ Family news outlet when the case broke, joined "Crime Junkie" host Ashley Flowers to discuss the note and a "wrench attack" theory that emerged in May.

A "Crime Junkie" spokesperson said the podcasters would not share copies of the text, which has not been independently verified. However, Whitney read excerpts on air:

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTE CLAIMED THAT SHE HAD DIED, BEEN ‘BURIED WITH NATURE,’ SOURCE SAYS

"Hello Savannah, We have your mother Nancy," she began, reading from what she said was the first note sent to local media. "She is safe but scared. She will be held for ransom, and once payment is received, she will be released unharmed. We will be holding her for a maximum of seven days."

As previously conveyed by authorities, the sender gave two deadlines with increased money demands the longer it took.

"Once payment is received to the Bitcoin address below, she will be released within 12 hours of deposit to a safe drop-off location back in Tucson," Whitney read. "If the payment is not received by the last deadline on Monday the 9th at 5 p.m., she will be killed. Your mother is aware of this, and her life is in your hands."

NANCY GUTHRIE RANSOM NOTES DON'T MATCH SUSPECT'S BEHAVIOR, PROFILER SAYS: 'I DON'T BELIEVE THEY'RE REAL'

The sender left no room for further dialogue.

FOLLOW THE FOX TRUE CRIME TEAM ON X

"It is in the best interest of everyone to have this completed as soon as possible," the note reportedly continued. "You will not be able to contact me from here on out. There will be no negotiation. Do not play games. Law enforcement will not be able to help you."

GOT A TIP?

The note also included details that may have only been available to investigators and the suspect at the time, including the location of Guthrie's Apple watch and details about the smashed floodlight in her backyard.

SAVANNAH GUTHRIE PLEADS FOR TIPS AS RANSOM NOTE CLAIMS MOM IS DEAD: 'SOMEBODY KNOWS SOMETHING'

She also read from a purported second note, which claimed that the kidnappers had underestimated Guthrie's health and that she had died shortly after being taken. That note reportedly included an apology at the end.

LISTEN TO THE NEW 'CRIME & JUSTICE WITH DONNA ROTUNNO' PODCAST

Guthrie was taken from her Tucson home in the early morning hours of Feb. 1, according to the Pima County Sheriff's Department and the FBI.

The only known suspect remains publicly unidentified — a masked intruder who appeared on Nest doorbell camera video that the FBI recovered with help from Google.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST TRUE CRIME NEWS

He is described as standing between 5 feet, 9 inches and 5 feet, 10 inches tall with an average build. He was wearing a black Ozark Trail backpack.

The sum of rewards in the case is over $1.2 million.

Anyone with information is asked to dial 1-800-CALL-FBI. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Tucson's Crime Stoppers affiliate at 520-88-CRIME.

item.title: GOP firebrand moves to dissolve Congressional Black Caucus: ‘Federally sanctioned segregation’
item.description: Nancy Mace's resolution would bar race-based House caucuses, effectively forcing dissolution of the Congressional Black Caucus and similar groups.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX: A Republican firebrand is pushing legislation to abolish some of the most influential Democratic groups in Congress.

Rep. Nancy Mace, R-S.C., introduced a resolution Thursday barring the formation of House caucuses organized around race, ethnicity or national origin.

If adopted, the measure would effectively force the dissolution of Democrat-aligned groups, including the Congressional Black Caucus, the Congressional Hispanic Caucus and the Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus.

The proposal could also affect GOP-affiliated organizations, including the Black Republican Congressional Staff Association, the GOP Latino Staff Association and the Republican Asian American Staff Association.

KEY TRUMP AGENCY PUTS TOP UNIVERSITIES IN CROSSHAIRS OVER ALLEGED RACE-BASED DORM PROGRAMS

"Race-based caucuses are not diversity. They are federally sanctioned segregation," Mace said in a statement to Fox News Digital. "You do not get to lecture America about inclusion while running an organization which checks your skin color at the door. The hypocrisy ends now."

"It is time to end taxpayer-funded discrimination in this institution," the South Carolina Republican added.

The proposal faces long odds in the Republican-controlled House, where it would likely encounter universal opposition from Democrats and some GOP lawmakers.

The resolution comes as Republicans' recent redistricting efforts in several Southern states have reduced the size of the Congressional Black Caucus by eliminating or reshaping multiple Black-majority districts represented by CBC members.

MACE SNUBS TRUMP'S PREFERRED SENATE CANDIDATE, BACKS RALPH NORMAN INSTEAD

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., who could become the first Black speaker if Democrats retake control of the chamber in November, is a member of the influential group.

Fox News Digital reached out to the Congressional Black Caucus, the Congressional Hispanic Caucus and the Congressional Asian Pacific American Caucus for comment.

It is unclear whether Mace, who is leaving office in January, would push for a chamber-wide vote.

Mace has spent her final months in Congress rolling out legislation targeting diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) policies, transgender participation in women's sports and birthright citizenship.

The South Carolina Republican opted against a bid for the Senate seat vacated by the late Sen. Lindsey Graham and recently endorsed Rep. Ralph Norman in the crowded GOP primary.

item.title: FCC chair suggests ABC skipping Trump's speech will factor in license renewal decision
item.description: FCC Chairman Brendan Carr suggested ABC's decision to skip President Donald Trump's primetime address could factor into the network's broadcast license renewals.
item.content:encoded:
Federal Communications Commission (FCC) Chairman Brendan Carr suggested this week that ABC's recent decision to skip President Donald Trump's primetime address could factor into the network's broadcast license renewals.

ABC, along with NBC, opted not to air the speech that addressed election security and apparent vulnerabilities during the 2020 election on linear television. CNN also skipped the address, but ABC and NBC are broadcast networks regulated by the FCC, while cable channels have more flexibility. 

"I was asked at the beginning of the week whether I thought broadcasters should air the president's remarks, and I said, of course they should," Carr told Fox News Digital. "And they should do so because they have an obligation at the end of the day to operate in the public interest to serve the needs of their communities. And I think making sure that people have access to significant presidential addresses is part and parcel of what all broadcasters should want to do."

While broadcast networks skipping a presidential primetime address is not unprecedented, Carr suggested that the saga might not go away anytime soon. 

ABC HAS NO BUSINESS ON PUBLIC AIRWAVES BECAUSE IT OPERATES 'LIKE A PARTISAN CABLE NETWORK,’ WATCHDOG TELLS FCC

"There's been a lot of interest in Congress. Obviously, we got a letter from Congress asking us to investigate the decisions to do this or not do this, and we're reviewing the letter itself, at least at this point. And there's been a lot of interest in this, so we'll see where it goes," Carr said.

Carr was referring to a letter from Rep. Buddy Carter, R-Ga., to the FCC on Monday calling on the agency to review broadcast networks' decisions and whether they violated their obligation to "serve the public interest."

"Broadcasters may criticize, analyze, or fact-check the President's statements," he wrote. "However, refusing to air a major presidential address because executives disagree with its content raises serious concerns that they intended to keep Americans in the dark about potential foreign interference in our elections."

Fox News Digital reached out to ABC's parent company, Disney, for comment but did not immediately receive a response. 

ABC FIRES BACK AT FCC PROBE OF 'THE VIEW,' CALLS OUT AGENCY'S 'CHILLING EFFECT ON FIRST AMENDMENT'

In additional comments to reporters on Wednesday, Carr added that the concerns over ABC not airing the speech could be brought up during ongoing investigations and proceedings on the network.

"I’m sure that there are going to be points raised in that proceeding that relate to Disney’s decision..." he said. "Again, we’re going to hold all broadcasters to the public interest obligations, and we’re always looking to make sure they do exactly that."

Carr called the licenses of eight ABC-owned stations for early renewal in April. The stations must prove they have been operating in the public interest as part of an ongoing look at Disney’s diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) practices. The Disney-owned ABC affiliates’ licenses were originally scheduled to be renewed between 2028 and 2031, but Trump’s FCC expedited the process. 

On Thursday, a group of 14 conservative organizations, led by the Center for Individual Freedom (CFIF), reiterated support for Carr's deregulatory efforts in the communications sector but respectfully urged the FCC to "preserve the well-established, routine, predictable, and presumptive nature of broadcast license renewals."

"Our concern is that establishing precedents of non-routine renewal creates a powerful lever that future FCCs — including ones hostile to conservative, religious, or otherwise disfavored broadcasters — would be all too willing to pull. The surest protection against that future is to keep the renewal process predictable today," they wrote. 

FCC CHAIRMAN CARR SAYS SPECTRUM AUCTION WILL MAKE WIRELESS FASTER, CHEAPER WHILE RAISING BILLIONS FOR TREASURY

The FCC also launched an investigation into network talk shows like ABC's "The View" over its alleged failure to provide equal treatment for political candidates. ABC has asserted that the FCC previously recognized "The View" as a "bona fide" news program in 2002, making it an exception to equal time rules.

ABC and NBC stations owned by Sinclair Broadcasting Group did preempt the networks and aired Trump's speech. 

Fox News Digital's Joseph A. Wulfsohn contributed to this report.

item.title: One of NFL's fastest players has 'no power' in one injured leg, casting doubts on 2026 season
item.description: Tyreek Hill reveals he has no power in his left leg after two surgeries, but new video shows the free agent sprinting again for the first time.
item.content:encoded:
The thing Tyreek Hill is known for, aside from his off-field troubles with domestic partners, is his elite sprinter's speed. But the two surgeries he's had since dislocating his left knee in September 2025 have changed that – at least for now.

"My left leg is the one that I injured," Hill said in a YouTube video released Wednesday titled "The Cheetah Runs Again." "I have no power in my left leg at all. I'm trying to regain all the power back in my left leg," he continued.

It is obviously not optimal for an NFL wide receiver who relies on speed and quickness to be essentially operating on one leg 10 months after an injury.

TYREEK HILL ACCUSED BY 250-POUND ONLYFANS MODEL OF BREAKING HER LEG, THEN GIVING HER A PIGGYBACK RIDE TO SEX

That is the reason no team signed Hill this offseason despite the fact the Miami Dolphins released him after 2025 and he is a free agent.

But Hill shows his trademark optimism in the video, discussing his approach to rehabilitation and suggesting he remains hopeful for an NFL return.

"I'ma keep grinding, dog. You know?" Hill said. "My timeline looking, I ain't gonna tell you what my timeline is, but timeline looking real good right now, man."

One NFL source said Hill's timeline is to be back in the league – preferably with a team pointed toward the playoffs – before the end of this season. That would allow him to perform in the postseason.

But even that goal may be too ambitious.

"Teddy Bridgewater dislocated his knee and he missed a year-and-a-half and he's a quarterback," said Dr. David Chao, the founder of Sports Injury Central, the former team doctor of the San Diego Chargers and OutKick's sports injury expert.

"The injury was more than an ACL. It was a knee dislocation with multiple ligaments. You talk about a true orthopedic emergency. And potential for, never been reported to my knowledge, nerve damage – and is that why his leg still doesn't have power?

"This is Herculean for a quarterback to return in one year. He's a wide receiver. Not going to happen."

Hill said his recovery has required two surgeries, the latest to address an LCL issue in February.

Hill's video apparently signaled the first time he ran on his rebuilt left limb although it included nothing more than a few sprints in his backyard and work in his pool.

"My knee's like, bro. What are you doing today?" Hill said. "You know, they told me I may not be able to walk. And the dog was like, bro, we don't know what's gonna happen with your future. And now look at me."

So, Hill believes he's on course for an NFL return at some point.

But he's obviously nowhere near the end of his journey. And he clearly knows this.

SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS

"Just trying to get my explosion, and my first step," he said. "So, whenever I step back on the field, it's like none of it is missing."

This week was merely a step to get some power back into his leg.

Follow Armando Salguero on X: @ArmandoSalguero

item.title: Capitol Hill sex scandals trigger new push to redraw the line for lawmakers
item.description: Speaker Mike Johnson endorsed new proposals banning lawmakers from dating congressional staff as multiple sexual misconduct cases shake Capitol Hill.
item.content:encoded:
Efforts to bar lawmakers from dating congressional staff are gaining momentum following multiple high-profile cases of alleged sexual misconduct on Capitol Hill.

Rep. Jeff Crank, R-Colo., introduced legislation on Tuesday that would bar members of the House of Representatives from having sexual relationships with any congressional staffers unless the House Ethics Committee determines the contact does not constitute a conflict of interest. The proposal follows a number of sexual controversies facing lawmakers such as former Rep. Eric Swalwell, D-Calif., former Rep. Tony Gonzales, R-Texas, Rep. Jimmy Gomez, D-Calif., and Sen. Ruben Gallego, D-Ariz.

A Gallego spokesperson told Fox News Digital the lawmaker supports the House bill and hopes to see Republicans who engage in relationships with staffers held accountable. 

Members of Congress are already prohibited from dating their personal employees or staff serving on their committees. Crank’s proposal would expand that prohibition to all congressional staff pending approval from the ethics committee.

'DISTURBING' ALLEGATIONS AGAINST UNNAMED SENATOR UNDER REVIEW IN WAKE OF SCANDALS ROCKING CONGRESS

Speaker of the House Mike Johnson, R-La., signaled that he is on board with the movement.

"I think it's inappropriate, as it would be in any workplace," Johnson told Axios on Tuesday when asked for his thoughts on relationships between representatives and staffers. "We should, at a bare minimum in Congress, have, you know, rules and traditions that are adhered to in corporate America, so that's an easy one for me."

During a press conference that same day, he endorsed establishing a new rule proposed by Rep. Lisa McClain, R-Mich., that would ban members from having sex with any congressional staffers.

"The overall reception from members to this resolution has been positive," Crank told Fox News Digital about his legislation. "I think that’s a great sign that my colleagues know we need to take serious action to hold others accountable. I’m looking forward to continuing conversations and working with anyone that is willing to be productive to get this passed."

SEX CRIMES COULD COST LAWMAKERS TAXPAYER-FUNDED PENSIONS UNDER HAWLEY’S NEW PROPOSAL
