"Even if a relationship is consensual, there is an inherent power imbalance that can undermine trust and create an appearance of impropriety," McClain wrote on social media. "The goal should be protecting staff and maintaining confidence in the institution."

The push among Republicans to impose new rules on workplace relationships came after the New York Post reported that Gallego had allegedly engaged in consensual sexual relationships with at least two House staffers employed by other Democratic lawmakers while he served in the House.

ARIZONA GOV HOBBS SIDESTEPS WHETHER SHE BELIEVES GALLEGO'S ANSWERS ON SWALWELL

While the senator did not deny the allegations, he stated that he would not "engage in gossip." Gallego’s alleged sexual relationships with staff occurred before he was married. 

Focus on this issue intensified in April when multiple women accused Swalwell, a longtime friend of Gallego, of unwanted sexual contact, including rape. Swalwell, who maintained his innocence but apologized for unspecified "mistakes in judgment," suspended his campaign for California governor and resigned from Congress after his accusers went public.

Gonzales, meanwhile, resigned from Congress in April after admitting to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide. Gonzales denied that the affair contributed to her death.

item.title: Ed Harris' biggest ‘Dutton Ranch’ complaint sparked a major shakeup: report
item.description: Ed Harris was reportedly involved in picking the new showrunner for "Dutton Ranch" season two after claiming he felt "underused and inconsequential" in season one.
item.content:encoded:
It seems as though Ed Harris' concerns surrounding his role in "Dutton Ranch" have been met.

According to Deadline, Harris, who recently shared he didn't have the best experience filming season one of the show, was kept in the loop and was involved in picking the show's new showrunner, Benjamin Cavell, after the show's creator and former showrunner, Chad Feehan, was let go after season one.

The decision to replace Feehan was "largely related" to how Harris' character was "underutilized," Deadline claimed.

The actor recently went viral when he shared he wanted to quit the show halfway through filming the first season, because he felt "underused and inconsequential."

‘DUTTON RANCH’ STAR ED HARRIS BLASTS PRODUCERS, SAYS HE WANTED TO VOID CONTRACT: ‘GET ME THE F--- OUT'

"Midway through this first season, I was ready to go; I said, ‘Get me the f--- out of here,’ to tell you the truth. Because I didn’t feel like I was being used," he told Variety. "I felt a little bit misled."

He continued, "Prior to signing on for it, I was talked to quite a bit about what the season was, and what my character was going to do — and that I was one of the four main characters. And that wasn't really the case."

One of the issues he mentioned was finding out a scene they filmed involving his character, veterinarian Everett McKinney, was cut from the show.

"So, there's this scene in there … I'm talking to [Beth Dutton] at the bar, and [Rip Wheeler] comes in and says, 'OK, Everett, come on, it's your turn,'" Harris said. "I walk up to the mic, and they cut it… They cut the song out. I'm going, 'F--- you, people.'"

Harris claims producers told him the musical number would have been "too 'up' for the dark ending of the episode," adding he felt that was "enough reason to say I'm voiding my contract."

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

He added that he took his concerns to production, but didn't mention Taylor Sheridan, the creator, writer and executive producer of "Yellowstone" and its spinoffs.

"I said, 'I'm feeling underused and inconsequential,' and they kind of went, 'Oh,'" Harris said. "I'm being told my character will have a little bit more to do [in Season 2]. I was just a little frustrated, to tell you the truth."

In the end, Harris admitted his perspective on his character changed when he watched the show, saying he thought the show was "pretty good," and that his character was still "relatively important to the story," although that wasn't how it felt while filming it.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

Despite his blunt remarks, Harris remains signed on for two seasons of "Dutton Ranch," and it remains unclear whether he is seriously considering leaving the series.

"Dutton Ranch" premiered on Paramount + in May and serves as a continuation of "Yellowstone" after that show ended in December 2024.

The series picks up with fan favorites Beth Dutton (Kelly Reilly) and Rip Wheeler (Cole Hauser) as they trade Montana for South Texas, where they're determined to build a ranch — and a new life — from the ground up.

Fox News Digital reached out to Paramount, Harris and Cavell for comment.

item.title: Khanna demands answers from CCP-linked company accused of undercutting US manufacturers
item.description: Rep. Ro Khanna demands Fuyao Glass reveal its CCP ties, labor practices and subsidies amid allegations of smuggling migrant workers to its Ohio plants.
item.content:encoded:
FIRST ON FOX – Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., sent a letter to the Chinese Communist Party (CCP)-linked Fuyao Glass Industry Group Co., one of the world’s leading automotive glass manufacturers with U.S. headquarters in Ohio, demanding information on the company’s operations and finances amid allegations of labor violations.

The letter, first obtained by Fox News Digital, asks for extensive documentation of Fuyao’s business operations in the U.S., its labor practices, its ties to the CCP and more.

Khanna, the top Democrat on the House Select Committee on the Strategic Competition Between the U.S. and the Chinese Communist Party, cited allegations that Fuyao has been violating U.S. labor laws by, among other things, importing foreign workers to undercut American labor.

"They have serious allegations against them of suppressing prices because they're being subsidized by the Chinese Communist Party, allegedly, of bringing people into this country who are undocumented and violating our immigration laws, and then exploiting those workers and having them work long hours with little pay," Khanna told Fox News Digital.

CHINA IS BUILDING A MASSIVE SHIP THAT SHOULD SCARE AMERICANS ABOUT THE FUTURE

A 2024 federal raid on Fuyao’s Ohio properties resulted in a civil asset forfeiture of $126 million that included the seizure of luxury goods, vehicles, and 12 commercial and residential properties, including what the U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) described as "boarding houses" to accommodate migrant workers.

"It is alleged that many of the workers were illegally smuggled into the United States, primarily through Mexico, and encouraged to travel to the Dayton area to be employed by one of the target entities and serve as a workforce at the various factories. Most of the workers are of Chinese or Hispanic nationality. Workers allegedly lived at 'family style hotels' (boarding houses) owned by the target entities and were driven to and from work in transportation provided by the target entities," the DOJ said in a press release.

CHILL COMING FROM TRUMP’S SUMMIT WITH XI IS PROOF OF A NEW COLD WAR WITH CHINA

Among the many pieces of information Khanna requested from Fuyao was data on whether the company was being subsidized by the CCP, allowing them to undercut U.S. competitors, who he argued were being negatively affected by Fuyao’s practices.

"They are hurting. American glass manufacturers, one of them that we met on our Heartland tour in Western Pennsylvania. And they are putting American workers out of business," Khanna told Fox News Digital. "And at the same time, they are exploiting our laws. Now we have requested the findings from the Justice Department that undertook an investigation. And they still have not released the findings. And so I am going to take on myself, with the China Committee, to investigate Fuyao."

In the 2025 press release, the DOJ said a criminal investigation into Fuyao is ongoing.

Khanna also asked the company to reveal the degree to which the CCP is involved in the company's operation.

"We need to understand a lot more. I want to understand whether they're getting subsidies from the CCP. I want to understand whether any CCP government official is on their board or on their executive team," he said. "I want to understand if they're providing reports to the CCP, I want to understand if the CCP is helping them to lower prices where they can undercut American competition. And I certainly want to know if the CCP is helping them smuggle in people who are undocumented. So these are unanswered questions that we need to answer."

Like all Chinese companies, Fuyao is required to have a CCP representative in their company ranks. The company's vice president, He Shimeng, is also a CCP secretary, according to the Made In China Journal.

HOUSE REPUBLICANS MOVE TO MAKE TRUMP'S BORDER POLICIES PERMANENT

When asked if the U.S.’s southern border’s porousness under former President Joe Biden relative to the border under President Donald Trump’s administration contributed to Fuyao’s ability to import foreign workers, Khanna said that a strong border is necessary. Notably, migrant encounters at the southern border are at a 50-year low under the second Trump administration, according to the Pew Research Center.

"I've said that we need border security, and I've also been candid that we allow too many people to come in without the proper vetting in 2024," Khanna told Fox News Digital.

"I disagree with the ICE raids that are taking place very strongly, but I believe we need a secure border," he said, adding, "as an American, we should be outraged that you have a Chinese-funded company that is undercutting American jobs, potentially exploiting workers, and potentially breaking our immigration laws. So with that. That is wrong. And so I am going to make sure that we stand up for American workers and for our laws."

Fuyao was the subject of the 2019 Netflix documentary "American Factory," which captured the company’s takeover of a former General Motors plant in Moraine, Ohio, a suburb of Dayton. One of the film’s major themes was Fuyao’s million-dollar effort to crush the area’s local unions.

"Once the focus begins to shift... to the fight over an upcoming vote on unionization — with [Fuyao founder] Cao [Dewang] paying 'consultants' over a million dollars to lecture workers at length on the horrors of unions and then telling them that 'you have a voice' — the film begins to toughen considerably as it shows how corporate manipulation works," Roger Ebert critic Peter Sobczynski wrote in 2019.

The alleged union-busting is a major focus of Khanna’s inquiry.

"What is Fuyao’s reasoning for its opposition to unionization efforts at facilities in the United States?" Khanna’s letter asked.

"Has Fuyao retaliated against any whistleblower (without regard to whether the subject matter of the whistleblower allegation related to collective bargaining), including but not limited to through actions impacting employment, hours, pay, employment records, or other workplace decisions?" Khanna also queried.

In addition to requesting the comprehensive information packet from Fuyao, Khanna is also requesting an onsite visit to the company's flagship Ohio plant before Nov. 1, "so I can go in and see for myself who the workers are," he told Fox News Digital.

"Are they undocumented? Are they being exploited, what the pricing is?" he asked.

"What we have heard is they've engaged in anti-union activity," Khanna told Fox News Digital. "What we've heard is that they are working their employees long hours well beyond the 40-hour work week. What we heard is, that they're not paying overtime. What we're heard is wage theft, that they're not paying their workers an appropriate wage. So all of this is exploitive. And of course, if these are undocumented workers in certain cases, they're most vulnerable. They're totally dependent on Fuyao for their staying in America and their living."

Fuyao is one of the largest automotive glass manufacturers and suppliers in the world. The company claims to have 35% of global market share in the automotive glass trade, a number corroborated by trade publications like Bamboo Works, which pegs the number even higher at 40%. Fuyao supplies glass to global and U.S. automakers such as BMW, Volkswagen, General Motors and Ford.

Khanna said that if Fuyao did not comply with his request for information, he would be willing to subpoena the information from them as ranking member of his committee.

"We can subpoena them, if not, and I fully expect to have this as a priority when I become chair, if we win the election, but we wanna do this in a bipartisan way," he said. "They need to allow us onto the site and they need to provide this information."

He explained that he has a good relationship with committee Chairman Rep. John Moolenaar, R-Mich., and said other colleagues across the aisle have supported his efforts.

"I fully anticipate it will be bipartisan. I have a very good relationship with John Moolenaar, our chair. We share a commitment to an American industrial policy. We understand that there needs to be more investment in our communities, and we share a concern about Chinese companies coming into the auto industry or Chinese companies coming in. If they're not playing fair, I don't want Ohio to be taken over by Chinese companies that are violating our trade laws and our labor laws and our immigration laws," he said.

Khanna conceded that Trump was one of the first to raise the issue of China's unbalanced relationship with the U.S., but still criticized the president's overall policy on China, giving it a "C" when asked to grade it.

"I'd give it a 'C' because I think he was the first to sound the alarm on China, eating our lunch and taking our jobs. And he was right to sound the alarm. And so I give him credit for sounding the alarm, but he hasn't followed through. Why are we begging them for foreign investment? Why aren't we having an industrial investment bank for our people here? Why aren't we investing in our industry and ships and steel and robotics here? And in building up our manufacturing base? And then why aren't we going after companies like Fuyao, where they're clearly violating the law?" Khanna said.

Fox News Digital contacted Moolenaar, Fuyao and the DOJ for comment.

item.title: WATCH: Brandon Gill corners law school gatekeeper with DEI question she won't answer
item.description: Rep. Brandon Gill pressed ABA president on whether diversity principles would justify rejecting a third Black Supreme Court justice nomination.
item.content:encoded:
Rep. Brandon Gill, R-Texas, put American Bar Association President Michelle A. Behnke in the hot seat Wednesday, repeatedly pressing her on whether the ABA's diversity principles would justify rejecting a third Black Supreme Court justice.

The tense exchange came during a House Oversight Committee Task Force on Defending Constitutional Rights and Exposing Constitutional Abuses hearing examining the ABA's role in law school accreditation. Republicans argued the organization has used its accreditation authority to advance race-conscious DEI policies throughout the legal profession, while the ABA defended its commitment to diversity, saying it strengthens the profession without supporting racial quotas.

Gill, who chairs the task force, has accused the ABA of using its accreditation power to pressure law schools into adopting race-conscious admissions policies and mandatory DEI-related coursework.

"DEI is a cancerous ideology that promotes unlawful discrimination and steals opportunities from Americans who would otherwise earn jobs and law school admissions spots on merit," Gill said ahead of the hearing.

CONGRESS TO PUT AMERICAN BAR ASSOCIATION IN HOT SEAT OVER DEI RULES TIED TO LAW SCHOOL ACCREDITATION

During one of the hearing's sharpest exchanges, Gill questioned Behnke about statements on the ABA's website promoting judicial diversity. He cited the ABA's position that judges should reflect the diversity of American society, then asked whether a racially representative Supreme Court would be beneficial.

"The ABA believes that diversity in all of its forms is important," Behnke responded, but repeatedly declined to give Gill a yes-or-no answer.

DISCRIMINATORY 'SHADOW' REVIEW PROCESS LOCKS WHITE, ASIAN STUDENTS OUT OF CALIFORNIA MED SCHOOL, DOJ ALLEGES

Gill followed up by asking whether the Supreme Court should "roughly resemble America's racial makeup."

"The ABA does not support quotas," Behnke replied.

"I'm not asking about quotas," Gill responded. "I'm asking if it should roughly resemble America's racial makeup."

"It should represent all of what people are," Behnke said.

Gill responded by noting that two of the Court's nine justices are Black, representing roughly 22% of the Court compared with about 13% of the U.S. population, and asked whether that meant the Senate should reject a third Black nominee based solely on race.

"ABA does not support quotas," Behnke said.

JACKSON SCOLDS COLLEAGUES IN SOLO DISSENT AFTER COURT JUMPS INTO ROUTINE POLICE-STOP CASE

Earlier in the hearing, Gill pressed Behnke over President Joe Biden's 2022 pledge to nominate the first Black woman to the Supreme Court, asking whether race had clearly been a factor in Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson's nomination.

He also asked whether Biden had improperly excluded other demographic groups from consideration.

"The ABA does not support discrimination of any sort," Behnke responded, without directly addressing Biden's selection criteria.

The ABA holds considerable sway over legal education because, in most states, graduating from an ABA-approved law school is the main way to qualify for the bar exam. While the ABA says its law school accreditation council operates independently from the broader organization, Republicans argued the two are far more connected than the ABA lets on.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Behnke defended the ABA's commitment to diversity throughout her testimony, arguing that it strengthens rather than weakens the legal profession.

"When we have diverse teams, our profession is stronger," she told lawmakers. "It enhances our ability to solve our clients' problems when we can tap into the varied experiences and perspectives of people from every background and every community."

The hearing comes as several states, including Texas, Florida, Alabama and Tennessee, have explored alternatives to relying exclusively on ABA accreditation for bar eligibility.
